one_line_takeaway: 3D point-cloud VLMs (PointLLM, GPT4Point) are easily disrupted by white-box adversarial perturbations (untargeted ASR up to ~94%) but are far harder to coerce into a specific attacker-chosen output (targeted ASR mostly 12-28%) than 2D VLMs, because their 3D encoder's latent space is irregular and non-smooth -- confirmed by an ablation showing latent-feature perturbation gives 3-4x higher targeted ASR than raw point-cloud perturbation.
key_finding: 3D VLMs are substantially vulnerable to untargeted adversarial attacks but notably more resistant to targeted attacks than their 2D VLM counterparts, which the authors attribute to an irregular, non-smooth 3D latent space. Concretely (3D Object Captioning benchmark, Table 1, clean baseline AGS: PointLLM 46.6, GPT4Point 48.4): untargeted Vision Attack drops AGS to 26.4 (PointLLM) and 9.9 (GPT4Point); untargeted Caption Attack drops AGS to 31.8 (PointLLM) and 11.2 (GPT4Point). Targeted attacks only raise AGS to 7.1-9.6 (PointLLM) and 17.5-27.7 (GPT4Point) from baselines of ~4, far below full semantic hijack. GPT4Point is consistently more vulnerable than PointLLM across both benchmarks and both attack types (higher Γ, higher ASR at matched CSD). Vision Attack generally outperforms Caption Attack, especially for targeted attacks and at higher distortion for untargeted attacks. Under the Dynamic Deformation Constraint strategy (Table 2), untargeted ASR reaches 82.6-93.7% (both models, both datasets) while targeted ASR remains much lower (12.1-20.8%). Ablation (Table 3) shows perturbing latent vision-encoder features yields 3-4x higher targeted ASR than perturbing raw point clouds directly (e.g., GPT4Point on Objaverse: 23.9% point-cloud vs. 84.5% latent-feature), pinpointing the 3D encoder's latent-space irregularity as the main bottleneck for targeted control. Ablation (Table 4) shows adding Gaussian smoothing (KSM+GSM) roughly halves targeted ASR versus keypoint-selection alone (KSM) (e.g., GPT4Point on Objaverse: 21.7% KSM vs. 14.9% KSM+GSM), indicating directional smoothing constraints hurt attack effectiveness more than magnitude constraints.
core_contribution: The paper presents the first systematic study of adversarial robustness for point-based 3D Vision-Language Models (3D VLMs), specifically PointLLM and GPT4Point. It proposes two complementary white-box, gradient-based attack strategies: (1) Vision Attack, which perturbs the intermediate high-dimensional vision-token features produced by the point encoder and projector using a cosine-similarity loss, targeting the vision-language alignment stage of training; and (2) Caption Attack, which directly perturbs the input point cloud to manipulate the model's final generated text output via an autoregressive cross-entropy loss, targeting the end-to-end instruction-tuning stage. Each attack has untargeted (degrade output relative to ground truth) and targeted (coerce output toward an attacker-chosen caption/category) variants. To keep perturbations imperceptible/realistic on point clouds, the authors adopt a perturbation-regularization module built on prior work (HiT-ADV keypoint selection + Gaussian smoothing), combining kernel loss, hide loss (curvature-adaptive), and Chamfer-distance loss. They also introduce a 'Dynamic Deformation Constraint' training-round strategy that tightens/relaxes the perturbation budget across rounds based on per-sample attack success, and a Γ = |ΔAGS/ΔCSD| metric to jointly measure semantic-disruption efficiency per unit of geometric distortion.
threat_model: White-box attacker with full access to model parameters (point encoder, projector, language decoder) of PointLLM-7B (Point-BERT encoder + Vicuna decoder) and GPT4Point-2.7B (Point-BERT encoder + Q-Former + OPT decoder). The attacker perturbs the input point cloud x by a bounded/regularized perturbation δ, either directly optimizing against intermediate vision-token features (Vision Attack) or against the final generated caption tokens (Caption Attack), under a joint objective minimizing an adversarial loss plus a distance/imperceptibility regularization term. Both untargeted goals (any deviation from ground-truth caption/category) and targeted goals (forcing a specific attacker-chosen caption or category, drawn from a different class) are evaluated. The paper explicitly notes it does NOT address black-box or query-limited attacks (left as future work) and does not evaluate proprietary/closed models due to lack of access.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) restricted access to proprietary/commercial 3D VLMs precludes comprehensive robustness evaluation beyond the two open-source models tested; black-box or limited-query attack settings are left to future work. (2) The gradient-based methods require end-to-end differentiability, making them non-trivial to extend to models lacking such pipelines, including many scene-level (as opposed to object-level) 3D VLMs. Additional caveats visible in the paper: results against the three cited defenses (SRS, SOR, DUP-Net) are reported only in supplementary material not covered by this extraction, so their magnitude could not be independently verified here; the paper also relies on GPT-4 as an automated judge for caption quality (AGS), an evaluation choice inherited from PointLLM's protocol rather than independently validated in this paper.

quantitative_results: Table 1 (captioning, clean AGS baseline): PointLLM 46.6, GPT4Point 48.4
Table 1: untargeted Vision Attack AGS -> PointLLM 26.4, GPT4Point 9.9
Table 1: untargeted Caption Attack AGS -> PointLLM 31.8, GPT4Point 11.2
Table 1: targeted Vision Attack AGS -> PointLLM 7.1, GPT4Point 17.5 (from baselines ~4)
Table 1: targeted Caption Attack AGS -> PointLLM 9.6, GPT4Point 27.7
Table 2 (Dynamic Deformation Constraint, untargeted ASR): PointLLM Vision 85.4% (ModelNet40)/91.4% (Objaverse); GPT4Point Vision 92.9%/93.7%
Table 2 (targeted ASR): PointLLM Vision 14.1%/15.5%; GPT4Point Vision 17.9%/20.0%

Table 3 ablation: latent-feature vs point-cloud perturbation targeted ASR -> PointLLM 48.9% vs 16.0% (ModelNet), 76.9% vs 20.4% (Objaverse); GPT4Point 67.6% vs 20.2% (ModelNet), 84.5% vs 23.9% (Objaverse) -- 3-4x gain from attacking latent features

Table 4 ablation: KSM+GSM vs KSM-only targeted ASR -> PointLLM 8.2% vs 15.6% (ModelNet), 9.4% vs 18.8% (Objaverse); GPT4Point 10.3% vs 18.1% (ModelNet), 14.9% vs 21.7% (Objaverse) -- roughly half ASR with full regularization
baselines_compared: w/o Attack (clean point cloud baseline in Table 1)
point-cloud-level perturbation vs latent-feature perturbation (Table 3 ablation)
